query: current CDC guidelines on Prevnar 13 usage in children

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: PCV13 is not routinely recommended for healthy children above 5 years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: For high-risk children of age group 2 to 5 years, a 2-dose series of PCV13 with 8 weeks intervals followed by 1 dose of PPSV23 at least 8 weeks after the last PCV13 dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: The minimum age is 6 weeks for pneumococcal conjugate vaccine-13 valent (PCV13) and 2 years for pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine 23 valent (PPSV23).

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Catch-up vaccination for Haemophilus influenza is not recommended for previously unvaccinated children above 5 years of age without risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: For routine immunization, the conjugate vaccine (PCV13) is used. A 4-dose series is recommended at 2, 4, 6, and 12 to 15 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Shared clinical decision-making is advised for PCV13 vaccination for healthy elderly individuals above 65 without risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: If PCV13 and PPSV23 need to be given, PCV13 is administered first. They should not be given together on the same day.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Pneumococcal Vaccination
retrieved_docs.content: For age groups 7 to 23 months, a 2-dose series is recommended with a 12 weeks interval and a second dose given after 12 months. For unimmunized healthy children aged 2 to 5 years, 1 dose is recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Clinical Significance -- Meningococcal Vaccine (serogroup A,C,W,Y vaccination)
retrieved_docs.content: The menACWY-D vaccine is given as a 2-dose series with 12-week intervals for 9 to 23 months age group and 8 weeks for more than 24 months age group. For kids with sickle cell disease, the MenACWY-D vaccine is administered after 24 months at least 4 weeks after completion of the PCV13 series.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: The catch-up immunization for Hepatitis A vaccine is recommended for all unvaccinated individuals aged 2 to 18.

retrieved_docs.content: If a DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 10 years of age for other reasons, it can be included as the booster dose in the CDC schedule. If the DTaP or Tdap vaccine is given at 7 to 9 years of age, we should not include it as the booster dose, and Tdap should be readministered at eleven to twelve years of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding and Application of CDC Immunization Guidelines -- Issues of Concern -- 2020 CDC Immunization Schedule Updates
retrieved_docs.content: Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV) doses may be counted toward the United States vaccination requirements. The OPV administered before April 1, 2016, should be counted.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1388
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a kombinované vakcíny (combined vaccine), indicating that it incorporates multiple antigens into a single immunization preparation. This categorization highlights the vaccine’s role in delivering protection against several diseases—such as diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis, and polio—through one combined formulation, which can simplify vaccination schedules and improve patient compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations are identified as a category that classifies itself, meaning the concept of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations is used to define and encompass the same set of preventive immunizations and inoculation practices. This self‑referential classification underscores that the term represents a distinct group of preventive interventions within medical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1607
retrieved_docs.content: The NICHD Terminology encompasses a range of medical concepts, including the influenza vaccine (vakcíny proti chřipce) and bacterial gastroenteritis (التهاب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِي جرثومي). Within its subsets, the terminology also contains the concept الهَلْوَسة and the condition حالة ارتباكية (confusional state). By incorporating both preventive interventions such as the influenza vaccine and disease entities like bacterial gastroenteritis, as well as specific clinical descriptors such as الهَلْوَسة and حالة ارتباكية, the NICHD Terminology provides a comprehensive framework for categorizing and referencing diverse health‑related topics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1431
retrieved_docs.content: Influenza, referred to in Arabic as الالتهاب الرئوي, is a viral respiratory infection that can lead to or be associated with pneumonia. The broader term الالتهاب الرئوي also includes specific forms such as التهاب رئوي وليدي, which denotes neonatal pneumonia, a distinct subtype of pneumonia occurring in newborn infants. Understanding that influenza may precipitate pneumonia and that neonatal pneumonia is a particular manifestation of pneumonia aids clinicians in identifying at‑risk patients, especially infants, and in applying appropriate preventive and therapeutic strategies.
